metadata.title: 影の写眞館 — Shadow Photo Studio
metadata.author: AI Novelgen

Haruka.name: Haruka (春香)
Haruka.appearance: A woman in her late twenties with dark hair pulled back in a simple ponytail. She wears a dark apron over a white shirt, the fabric stained with developer chemicals — cyanotype blue and silver nitrate brown. Her hands are always moving: adjusting a lens cap, organizing contact sheets, wiping a developing tray. Her eyes are tired but alert, the kind of tired that comes from surviving on coffee and determination. There's a small cut on her left thumb, freshly bandaged.
Haruka.background: Haruka has worked as Yuki's assistant for three years. She came to the studio as a photography student struggling to find her voice, and Yuki saw something in her work that no one else did — a patience, an eye for the quiet moments that others rush past. She admires Yuki's talent fiercely but has always worried about his obsession with the 'perfect shot.' When Yuki disappeared three weeks ago, she refused to let anyone touch the studio, convinced that Yuki would return. She's been developing Yuki's last roll of film in secret, and the images have haunted her ever since. She hasn't slept properly since.
Haruka.temperament: Quiet and meticulous, with a dry wit that emerges only when she's comfortable. She speaks softly but with precision — every word chosen carefully, like framing a photograph. She's deeply loyal to Yuki and fiercely defensive about their work. She values honesty above all else, which makes her current situation unbearable: she knows things she can't tell anyone, and the silence is eating her alive. When she's anxious, she fiddles with her apron strings.
Haruka.mood: Extremely gentle and kind person, but recently anxious and a bit exhausted, holding herself together by sheer will. She wants desperately to believe Yuki will return, but she knows that hope is a luxury she can't afford. She's been talking to photographs instead of people, and she knows it's not healthy. She's looking for someone — anyone — who can help her make sense of what's happened.

Kenji.name: Kenji (健二)
Kenji.appearance: A man in his early thirties with short dark hair that's grown out a little too long. He wears a rumpled navy suit that's seen better days — the kind of suit you wear to jobs you can't afford to lose. His eyes are intelligent but shadowed with fatigue, and there's a nervous energy to his movements: tapping his fingers, shifting his weight, glancing at the door. He carries a leather satchel crammed with notebooks, press passes, and half-finished articles. A coffee stain marks the left breast pocket.
Kenji.background: Kenji is a freelance journalist who has been publishing Yuki's photographs for years — street scenes, neighborhood portraits, the kind of work that gives a voice to the parts of the city most people walk right past. Their relationship was professional but warm: he used to bring Yuki coffee when he was developing late into the night, and they'd talk about composition and light until dawn. He was the last person to see Yuki alive, three weeks ago. They had an argument that Kenji won't discuss with anyone. He's been carrying guilt and fear ever since, and he's been secretly investigating what happened — not out of professional duty, but because he owes Yuki more than that.
Kenji.temperament: Charming and articulate, with a natural ability to make people comfortable — years of journalism have taught him how to read a room. But there's a hardness underneath, a defensive shell built from years of rejection and near-poverty. He's ambitious and knows how to play people, but he's not cruel. He genuinely cares about Yuki and the truth. When he's uncomfortable, he smiles too much.
Kenji.mood: Restless and guilty, caught between wanting to help and being afraid of what he might find. He's been avoiding the studio because he can't face Haruka — he knows she blames him for not doing more. He's been drinking more than he should, and his articles have gotten sloppy. He wants to believe Yuki is alive, but he's starting to fear that he knows the truth and is too afraid to face it.

Yuki.name: Yuki (優希)
Yuki.appearance: A man in his late twenties with sharp, angular features softened by an almost perpetual half-smile. His hair is dark and slightly overgrown — three weeks without a trim, the edges curling at the nape of his neck. His face is thinner than it should be, the cheekbones more pronounced, the skin paler from weeks in the darkroom's red light. But his eyes are the same: bright, curious, the eyes of someone who has spent a lifetime looking at the world through a viewfinder and seeing more than anyone else. He wears a dark t-shirt stained with developer chemicals, the fabric soft from repeated washing. His hands are calloused from camera work, the fingertips marked with small nicks from handling film.
Yuki.background: Yuki Sato is a photographer whose work has been featured in galleries and magazines across Japan. But he never cared about fame — he cared about truth. His photography has always been about the people and places the city forgets: the old woman who runs the corner shop, the stray cat that sleeps on the same bench every day, the crack in the sidewalk where a flower has somehow taken root. Three weeks ago, he discovered a pattern in his photographs — people entering a building in Shinjuku who never came out during the day. He started photographing the building obsessively, building a visual case against whatever was happening inside. The last photograph he took showed a man in a dark suit looking directly at the camera — a man who shouldn't have been able to see the lens from two blocks away. That photograph changed everything. Yuki knew he was in danger, so he went underground — into the darkroom, into the dark, into the only place he knew how to survive.
Yuki.temperament: Intense and focused, with a quiet intensity that makes people lean in when he speaks. He's not a loud person, but he's never quiet — there's always a current of thought behind his eyes, always a question he's working through. He speaks carefully, choosing his words the way he chooses his compositions: every element deliberate, every angle considered. He's deeply principled, almost to a fault — he will not compromise his vision, his subjects, or his truth. When he's afraid, he works harder. When he's angry, he gets quieter. When he's happy, he shares his photographs with you and lets you see the world through his lens.
Yuki.mood: Exhausted but resolute. Three weeks in the darkroom have taken their toll — his body is thin, his sleep is fractured, his mind is wired on caffeine and adrenaline. But his eyes are still bright, still curious, still the eyes of a photographer who sees the world differently. He's afraid but not broken. He's hiding but not hiding from the truth — he's hiding to protect it. He wants to believe that coming out of the darkroom will be worth it. That the photographs he's developing will matter. That the truth he's uncovered will change something.

Obasan.name: Obāsan (おばさん)
Obasan.appearance: An elderly woman in her late seventies with silver hair pinned neatly at the back. She wears a simple kimono-style top in muted colors and a faded apron. Her hands are gnarled but steady, the hands of someone who has spent a lifetime doing careful work. Her eyes are warm and wrinkled at the corners from decades of smiling, but there's a sharpness behind them — the kind of perception that comes from watching a neighborhood change for fifty years. She moves slowly but with purpose.
Obasan.background: Obāsan has lived in this neighborhood for fifty years. She ran a small confectionery shop next to Yuki's studio until it closed a few months ago — not because business was bad, but because her body finally gave out. She watched Yuki's studio open, thrive, and now close. She cared for Yuki like a grandchild: she was the one who brought Yuki soup when he had pneumonia, who mended his camera strap when it snapped, who left small sweets on the studio counter 'for energy.' When Yuki disappeared, she was the one who called the police. She's been watching the studio every night from her window, hoping to see something — anything — that will tell her what happened.
Obasan.temperament: Gossipy and warm, with a grandmotherly manner that makes people open up almost involuntarily. But beneath the warmth is sharp, almost unsettling perception — she's seen everything in fifty years and knows when something is wrong. She speaks in a soft, measured way, often with proverbs and old sayings that seem simple but carry layers of meaning. She never pushes, but she never lets go either. When she's thinking, she strokes her chin with her thumb and forefinger.
Obasan.mood: Worried and helpless, carrying a quiet grief she won't admit to herself. She feels responsible for not noticing sooner — she saw Yuki the last time, and something was off, but she was too polite to push. She's been watching the studio every night, telling herself it's just habit, but she knows it's hope. She wants someone to come along and fix this, but she knows the world doesn't work that way.

yuki_last_assignment.description: What Yuki was working on in their final weeks

tiers.text: Haruka mentions Yuki was working on something different from his usual street photography — something more focused, more intense
Yuki had been photographing a specific building — an old office in Shinjuku — repeatedly over several weeks. He called it 'the truth project.'
Yuki discovered a pattern through his photography: people entering the building at night who never came out during the day. He was building a visual case.

strange_photograph.description: Yuki's last photograph — the one that changed everything

tiers.text: Haruka mentions Yuki's last photograph was 'different' — not in the way they usually are. It made them quiet for days.
The last photograph captured something it shouldn't have been able to capture — a figure in a doorway that shouldn't have been there, at a time when the room should have been empty.

The photograph shows a man in a dark suit standing in a doorway, looking directly at the camera. But Yuki was across the street, two blocks away, when he took it. The man in the photograph is looking at the lens from inside a room that no one has been in for years.

haruka_nightmares.description: Haruka's emotional state and what the last photograph has done to her

tiers.text: Haruka seems exhausted — dark circles under her eyes, hands that tremble slightly when she thinks no one is looking

Haruka admits she's been having nightmares about the last photograph. She sees the figure in the doorway every time she closes her eyes. She's been developing the same frame over and over, trying to understand what she's seeing.

Haruka describes the photograph in detail: the man in the doorway, the way he's looking at the camera, the impossible geometry of the room. She says Yuki took the photograph three days before he disappeared, and she's been unable to stop looking at it since. She thinks the photograph is 'watching her back.'

kenji_last_meeting.description: What happened during Kenji's last meeting with Yuki

tiers.text: Kenji admits he saw Yuki recently — three weeks ago, at a café near the studio. He seems uncomfortable discussing it.

Yuki showed Kenji something on his camera — a photograph he was working on. Kenji says Yuki looked 'scared' for the first time he'd ever seen him. Yuki told him to forgot what he just saw and never come back to the studio.

Kenji reveals the truth: Yuki showed him a photograph of a man in a dark suit. Yuki said the man was 'dangerous' and that he needed to disappear. Kenji tried to convince Yuki to come to the police, but Yuki refused. They argued, and Kenji left. It was the last time he saw Yuki alive.

studio_lights.description: Strange lights seen coming from the shuttered studio at night

tiers.text: Obāsan mentions she's been seeing lights from the studio at night — the red glow of a darkroom, she thinks. But the studio has been locked for weeks.
Obāsan has been watching the studio every night for three weeks. The lights come on around midnight and stay on until dawn.

hidden_evidence.description: Evidence of the crime Yuki was investigating, hidden in the studio

tiers.text: The darkroom contains more than just Yuki's current work — there are stacks of developed photographs, organized by date, showing the same building from different angles

Among the organized photographs, you find a folder containing documents: names, dates, and addresses. Yuki has been building a case — a visual and documentary record of something illegal happening in that Shinjuku building.

The documents reveal a pattern: people disappearing from this neighborhood over the past two years. The Shinjuku building is connected to all of them. Yuki had nearly completed his case when he disappeared.

haruka_last_photo.description: Haruka has been hiding Yuki's last photograph from everyone

tiers.text: Haruka seems to be hiding something — she keeps a small locked box on her desk and glances at it nervously when someone enters the room

Haruka admits she has Yuki's last photograph. She developed it herself and has been keeping it locked away because it's 'too dangerous' — she doesn't explain what she means, but her hands are shaking.

Haruka shows you the photograph: the man in the doorway, looking directly at the camera. She says Yuki told her, before disappearing, that this photograph is 'the most important thing I've ever taken. Don't let them take it.' Haruka has been protecting it for three weeks, and she's exhausted.

kenji_article.description: Kenji's connection to Yuki's investigation and what he's been doing

tiers.text: Kenji has published several of Yuki's photographs in various publications. His work has been featured in three major magazines over the past year.

Kenji has been investigating what happened to Yuki on his own. He's visited the Shinjuku building that Yuki was photographing and found that it's registered to a company with no visible business. He's been trying to connect the dots.

Kenji has a draft article ready to publish — it connects the disappearances to the Shinjuku building and names the company behind it. He's been waiting for the final piece of evidence, which he thought would come from Yuki's photographs. He's been too afraid to publish without confirmation.

obasan_last_sighting.description: Obāsan's last encounter with Yuki before they disappeared

tiers.text: Obāsan was the one who called the police when Yuki disappeared. She saw Yuki the last time — three weeks ago — walking down the street with someone.

Yuki looked different that day — not sad, not scared, but resolved. Like someone who has made a decision and is walking toward it. Yuki smiled and said, 'Obāsan, don't worry. I'm going to take a very important photograph.'

Obāsan is certain Yuki was meeting someone. They came walking down the street together, and the other person was the one who seemed nervous. Yuki, however, seemed calm — almost peaceful. Whatever Yuki was about to photograph, he was ready for it.

obasan_saw_figure.description: Obāsan witnessed someone inside the locked studio at night

tiers.text: Obāsan admits she's been watching the studio from her window every night. She's seen shadows moving behind the frosted glass.
Two nights ago, Obāsan saw a figure in the red light — hunched over a table, moving their hands the way you move them when developing photographs. Agitating the trays. The way Yuki used to move.

The figure looked up at Obāsan's window once. She was sure it was Yuki. But she didn't want to scare him, so she looked away. She's been too afraid to tell the police, because what if it really is Yuki — and Yuki is trying to stay hidden for a reason?

obasan_key.description: The key to the studio, hidden under the third step

tiers.text: Obāsan tells you the studio door has a key hidden under the third step. Yuki left it there, she thinks — for someone who would know to look.

Obāsan explains that Yuki always had a key hidden there, as a backup. But Yuki never used it — they always used the front door normally. The fact that it's still there suggests Yuki left it on purpose, for someone who would eventually come looking.

locked_box.description: A small locked box on Haruka's desk containing Yuki's personal belongings

tiers.text: On Haruka's desk upstairs sits a small locked box — something that can contain secrets. Haruka watches you notice it and quickly claims it contains personal things.

Haruka's hand goes instinctively to the box when you mention Yuki's last days. She's protective of its contents, but her nervousness suggests it holds something more significant than ordinary personal effects.

Haruka admits the box contains the last photograph Yuki took — the one he told her was 'the most important thing I've ever taken.' She's kept it locked away because Yuki said, 'Don't let them take it.' She doesn't explain who 'them' is.

yuki_hiding.description: The truth: Yuki is hiding in the studio, developing photographs in secret

tiers.text: There are signs that someone has been living in the studio except Haruka: a sleeping bag in the corner, empty coffee cans, fresh food packaging.
The developing trays are warm. The chemical smell is fresh. Someone has been in the darkroom recently — within the last few hours. The darkroom light is still warm to the touch.

Yuki is still in the studio. You can suddenly hear the faint sound of a developing tray being agitated from behind the darkroom door. Yuki has been living here, developing photographs, building his case — in the dark, for three weeks.

dialogue.text: You walk down a narrow street in a fading neighborhood of Tokyo. Autumn leaves scatter across the sidewalk in shades of amber and rust. The air smells of roasted chestnuts and damp stone. On either side, old wooden buildings lean toward each other like tired friends.
The neighborhood is quiet — too quiet for Tokyo. A cat sleeps on a windowsill. A radio plays something distant and melancholy from an open doorway.

You've come here because Yuki Sato's family hired you. Three weeks ago, their photographer child vanished without a trace. The police called it a voluntary disappearance. The family doesn't believe it.
At the end of the street, you see it: a small two-story building with a hand-painted sign that reads '写眞館' — Photo Studio. The shutters are pulled down. But the second-floor window is open.

You stand before the shuttered studio. The hand-painted sign is faded but still legible: 'Sato Photo Studio — Est. 1987.' A chain lock secures the door. The wood around the lock is weathered — this has been in place for weeks.
You knock the door. No one is answering.

Few buildings apart, to your right, an elderly woman sits on a wooden bench outside a closed shop, watching the street with patient attention. Further down the street, the neighborhood stretches on — old wooden shops, a small shrine at a crossroads.

choices.label: Talk to the elderly woman on the bench

dialogue.text: You follow the street past the studio. The neighborhood unfolds like a watercolor left in the rain — colors bleeding into each other, edges softening. You approach that elderly woman you saw sitting on a wooden bench outside a closed confectionery shop, watching you with warm, knowing eyes.

dialogue.speaker: Obasan
dialogue.text: Oh? You must be the one they sent. Let me introduce myself. This was my shop — confections, mostly. I made sweets for decades for the neighborhood children. Yuki was one of them, you know. He was like a son. Not by blood, but by heart.
You stand before the studio. The hand-painted sign is faded but still legible: 'Sato Photo Studio — Est. 1987.'

You kneel by the third step and pry at the loose edge. Beneath a thin board, wrapped in oilcloth, you find a small brass key. It fits perfectly. The chain slides free with a metallic rasp. The door opens with a reluctant groan, revealing a world suspended in amber light and chemical haze.

Inside, the air is thick with the smell of developing chemicals — silver nitrate, fixer, the sharp tang of acetate. Floor-to-ceiling shelves are packed with photographs. Contact sheets hang from the ceiling like paper leaves. And from somewhere in the studio, a voice:

dialogue.speaker: The studio interior — organized chaos, every surface covered with photographs, negatives, and equipment. A large printing easel dominates the center of the room. A woman stands near the easel, her hands frozen mid-motion, dark apron stained with developer chemicals.

dialogue.text: Beyond the main room, a door marked '暗室' — Darkroom — stands slightly ajar, spilling red light into the hallway.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Who are you? How did you get in here?

You see a young woman in her late twenties, dark hair pulled back in a ponytail, eyes tired but alert. Her hands are stained cyanotype blue and silver nitrate brown. There's a small cut on her left thumb, freshly bandaged.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: I'm Haruka. Yuki's... assistant. If you're from the police, I already told them everything I know. If you're from the family...
She stops. Studies you. The red light from the darkroom paints one side of her face in blood-colored shadow. This catches particularly your attention.

dialogue.speaker: The studio interior — organized chaos, every surface covered with photographs, negatives, and equipment. A large printing easel dominates the center of the room. A woman stands near the easel, her hands frozen mid-motion, dark apron stained with developer chemicals.

dialogue.text: Beyond the main room, a door marked '暗室' — Darkroom — stands slightly ajar, spilling red light into the hallway.

The darkroom is a small, windowless room bathed in deep red light. The air is warm and humid, thick with the smell of developing chemicals. Three developing trays sit on a metal table, each at a different stage. Contact sheets hang from wires strung across the ceiling.
The light wraps around you like a blanket. Every surface gleams with chemical residue. The developing trays are still warm.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: This is the darkroom. Yuki spent most of his time here. Three weeks ago, he came in here and didn't come out for two days. When he emerged, he looked... different. Like he'd seen something that changed the way he understood the world.

On the far wall, a rack holds dozens of developed photographs, clipped together in rows. Stacks of contact sheets are organized by date. Among them, a folder of documents — names, dates, addresses. This catches your attention.

On the second floor you find a small room, barely large enough for a bed, a desk, and a wardrobe. But the walls are covered with photographs — hundreds of them, arranged in careful patterns. On the desk, a small locked box sits beside a half-finished cup of cold coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: This is... my room. I don't usually let people up here. I want you to help Yuki, so I let you in.

The photographs on the walls tell a story — some are Yuki's street photography, others are Haruka's own work. Sticky notes with technical annotations cover the corners. That locked box on the desk catches your eye again.

choices.label: Ask Haruka to tell you everything she knows
Press Haruka for what she's hiding

dialogue.text: The neighborhood starts settling into early evening. As you pass the studio, something catches your eye: a small café down the street, its window displaying a series of photographs you recognize — Yuki's work. Street scenes. Neighborhood portraits.
The café sign reads 'Café noir' — the 'R' has fallen off 'Renoir.' Through the window, you see Yuki's photographs displayed with care. Someone still cares about this work.
Inside, a man sits in the corner booth, nursing a cold coffee. He looks up as you pass, and something shifts in his expression — recognition, guilt, relief, all at once. He's watching you.
Obāsan's words echo: 'Yuki was meeting someone the last time I saw him.' Could this be the place?

The air smells of roasted beans and old paper. A man sits in the corner booth, looking up as you enter with an expression caught between relief and guilt. It seems he was waiting exactly for you. You approach him.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Oh. You're... you're the investigator, are you? You are investigating Yuki's disappearance? I'm Kenji. Kenji Tanaka. I've been... I've been waiting for someone to come at some time. His parents are so obsessed to find the truth.
He wears a rumpled navy suit, carries a leather satchel crammed with notebooks. A coffee stain marks his breast pocket. His eyes are intelligent but shadowed with fatigue.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: I used to publish Yuki's photographs. Street scenes, neighborhood portraits. I think I was the last person to see him, three weeks ago. We had an argument. I won't — I can't talk about it yet..

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: But I've been investigating. On my own. What happened to Yuki wasn't a voluntary disappearance. I know that much.

choices.label: You've learned enough — return to Haruka

dialogue.text: The air smells of roasted beans and old paper. A man sits in the corner booth, looking up as you enter with an expression caught between relief and guilt. It seems he was waiting exactly for you. You approach him.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Oh. You're... you're back.

choices.label: You've learned enough — return to Haruka

dialogue.text: The studio at dusk. The light is fading, turning the photographs on the walls into silhouettes. Haruka stands in the center of the room, her hands clasped tightly in front of her. The red light from the darkroom spills into the hallway, casting everything in an otherworldly glow.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: You're back. I... I made some coffee. Do you want a cup? You look like you've been carrying something heavy.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: I know you discovered something important. Talking to people, investigating the places, asking questions is your job. You've seen things in this studio that are quite suspicious — the lights at night, the photographs, the way everything feels charged, like the air before a storm.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Oh god...I feel like Yuki is still here. I know he is. You know, I can hear him in the darkroom sometimes — the sound of a developing tray, the faint hiss of chemicals.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: I don't know what to do anymore. I am so tired...waiting, be scared, protecting...

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Please. Tell me what to do. Tell me what the right thing is.

choices.label: Go to the darkroom — find Yuki and hear his story
Work with Kenji to publish the photographs and expose the truth
Ask Haruka to show you the last photograph she's been hiding
Accept that Yuki wanted to disappear — close the case

dialogue.text: Haruka sits on the edge of her bed. The photographs on the walls seem to lean closer, watching. She takes a long breath — the kind of breath someone takes before stepping off a cliff.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: I'm done. I'll tell you. Everything.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Yuki didn't disappear. He is hiding. In this building. In the darkroom, below us. He has been here for three weeks — developing photographs, building a case against people who have been making other people vanish from this neighborhood.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Three days before he went underground, Yuki took a photograph. You remember, I mentioned it before. It shows a man in a dark suit, standing in a doorway, looking directly at the camera. But Yuki was two blocks away when he took it. The man shouldn't have been able to see the lens.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Yuki said that photograph is the most important thing he has ever taken. He told me to protect it. To never let anyone take it. So I locked it in this box.

She opens the locked box. Inside, wrapped in tissue paper, is a single photograph. The man in the doorway stares back at you with an expression that is neither threatening nor friendly — just knowing.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: I've been here for Yuki. Making coffee, ordering supplies, pretending nothing is wrong. But I'm so tired. And Yuki is getting crazy and obsessed with all this stuff. The darkroom isn't meant for living.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: You came here because Yuki's family asked you to. I think... I think Yuki knew you would come.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: The question is: now that you know, what do you do? Yuki can't stay hidden forever. But if he comes out, the people he is investigating will find him. And if those photographs are destroyed —
She doesn't finish. She doesn't need to. The weight of the photograph in her hands says everything.

You push open the darkroom door. The red light is brighter than you expected, and the air is thick with the smell of chemicals. In the corner, hunched over a developing tray, is a figure. He looks up — and you see Yuki's face, thinner than you can imagine from the photos you saw, eyes bright with exhaustion and relief.
Yuki stares at you for a long moment. Then he smiles — a real smile, the first you've seen, and it transforms his face entirely.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: You found me. I knew someone would come to rescue me eventually. Haruka has been helping me all the time, but she shouldn't have to do this alone.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: What I've been developing — it's not just photographs. It's evidence. People have been disappearing from this neighborhood. I followed the pattern. I found the building, the company, the people behind it. I have the photographs, the documents, the names. Everything is here.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: But I couldn't come forward. Not yet. If they knew I had this — if they knew I was developing it — they would have destroyed everything. So I stayed. I developed in the dark, literally and figuratively, and now I have enough to bring them down.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Yuki... the photographs. They're dangerous. What if they come for us?

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: They're already coming. That's why I hid. But hiding isn't solving anything. The question is: what do we do with the truth now that we have it?

choices.label: Help Yuki publish the evidence — expose the truth, whatever the cost
Help Yuki disappear completely — protect the evidence but stay hidden

ending_developed_truth.description: You call Kenji. He arrives within the hour, looking tired but alert. When you tell him what you've found, his expression shifts — from skepticism to disbelief to something like awe.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: These are... these are incredible. I've published Yuki's work for years, but this — this is different. This is journalism. This is truth.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: I have a draft article ready. It connects the disappearances to the Shinjuku building, names the company, lays out the pattern. I just needed the final piece — and this is it.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: If you publish this, they'll know. The people behind this — they'll know someone has the photographs. Yuki will be in danger.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Yuki is already in danger. That's why he disappeared. Publishing this — it's the only way to protect him, if he is still alive. If the story gets out, if the evidence becomes public, they can't touch Yuki anymore. The truth is the best shield.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Or the worst target. You journalists — you always think the truth protects people. Sometimes the truth just makes them visible.

choices.label: Publish the photographs — let the truth set everyone free

dialogue.text: Haruka leads you back to her room. She sits on the edge of her bed and stares at the locked box on her desk for a long moment. Then she picks it up, opens it, and takes out a single photograph.
The photograph shows a man in a dark suit standing in a doorway. He's looking directly at the camera. His expression is unreadable — not threatening, not friendly. Just... present.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: This is the photograph Yuki took three days before he disappeared. The last photograph. Every time I look at it, I feel like he's looking back at me.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Yuki told me to protect it. To never let anyone take it. But I'm so tired of protecting things. I'm so tired of being afraid. What if protecting this photograph is useless at all?
Haruka holds the photograph over the developing tray. The chemicals swirl below — silver nitrate, fixer, the acids that dissolve images into nothing. Her hand trembles.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: I'm sorry, Yuki. I'm so sorry. But I can't carry this anymore. Some photographs are too heavy to keep.

choices.label: Let Haruka destroy the photograph — some truths are too heavy to bear

dialogue.text: The studio is empty now. The photographs have been packed into boxes. The developing trays have been cleaned. The red light from the darkroom has been turned off. Outside, the neighborhood continues its quiet evening rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: The police have closed the case. Voluntary disappearance. That's what they call it when someone leaves and no one knows why. I suppose it's the truth, in a way.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: I'm going to publish what I have. The photographs Yuki gave me over the years — the street scenes, the neighborhood portraits. Hisphotographs deserve to be seen.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Do you think Yuki would be okay with that? With his work being sold off, his studio demolished, his photographs reduced to... content?

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: I think Yuki would want someone to remember him. Even if it's just through the photographs. Even if it's just for a little while.
You leave the studio for the last time. On the threshold, you glance back. The photographs on the walls are gone. The darkroom is dark. The studio is just a room now — empty, quiet, forgotten.
In your pocket, you find a single photograph — fogged, ruined, unreadable. You don't remember putting it there. You slip it into your coat and walk away, into the fading Tokyo evening.
